food_subgroup: Deep-fried whole fish with sweet and sour sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish specifically. Mapped to closest parent 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor. An exact term for this Suzhou preparation does not exist in the FoodOn ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Suzhou preparation. 'Fish, freshwater, cooked' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor node. The dish involves deep-frying mandarin fish (Siniperca chuatsi) with starch coating and sweet-sour sauce, which is not individually indexed in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific preparation. SNOMED CT does not index regional Chinese banquet dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish is approximately 300 g, representing a generous individual portion of the whole scored and deep-fried fish smothered in sweet and sour sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mandarin, cooked; adapted with deep-frying and sweet-sour sauce composition adjustments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for freshwater fish (cooked), adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption (approximately +3–4 g fat per 100 g), starch coating contribution to carbohydrates, and sweet-sour sauce sugar and sodium load. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained where they align with the USDA-anchored estimate. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.8 g per 100 g based on typical frying oil composition (soybean/vegetable oil). Cholesterol estimated at approximately 55 mg per 100 g based on USDA freshwater fish data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on battered/crumbed fried fish with sweet sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: battered/crumbed fried fish (GI ~55–65) combined with the high-sugar sweet-sour sauce component (sucrose-based sauce raises effective GI). The starch coating (cornstarch/potato starch) contributes rapidly digestible carbohydrate. Protein and fat from the fish and frying oil moderate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per full 300 g serving calculated as: (62 × 16.0 g net carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 29.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg per 100 g; approximately 960 mg per 300 g serving) from seasoned sweet-sour sauce and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~960 mg per serving, representing ~42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); if consumed, reduce sauce intake and pair with unsalted low-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycemic impact from starch-coated deep-fried fish combined with a high-sugar sweet-sour sauce (6.5 g sugar per 100 g; GI ~62)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish with caution: the starch coating and sucrose-rich sauce produce a significant postprandial glucose rise; portion control (half-serving), reduced sauce, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables are recommended to moderate glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (11.5 g per 100 g) with saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from mandarin fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish is acceptable in small portions occasionally; the fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but the deep-frying method and high sodium content warrant limiting frequency and serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-frying and high sugar content, partially offset by anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids in mandarin fish
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, but the deep-frying process and high sugar content in the sauce may promote inflammation; consume occasionally and in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish (松鼠桂鱼, Sōngshǔ guìyú) is an iconic showpiece dish of Jiangsu (Suzhou) cuisine and one of the most celebrated preparations in classical Chinese gastronomy. The dish is made from a whole mandarin fish (Siniperca chuatsi), which is meticulously deboned and then deeply scored in a fine crosshatch pattern across the flesh without cutting through the skin. When the fish is submerged in hot oil and deep-fried, the scored flesh fans outward dramatically, creating a texture resembling the fur of a squirrel, while the head and tail are positioned to complete the animal silhouette. The finished fish is immediately smothered in a glossy, vibrant sweet and sour sauce made from tomatoes, rice vinegar, sugar, and soy sauce, and garnished with pine nuts and shrimp. The result is a dish of extraordinary textural contrast: an intensely crispy, golden exterior giving way to tender, succulent white fish flesh inside. The flavour profile is a perfectly balanced interplay of sweet, sour, and savoury notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 215 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 16.5 g carbohydrate, with notable selenium (25.5 mcg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), and sodium (320 mg). The high sodium and sugar content make it a dish to be consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is traditionally served at celebratory banquets and formal dinners in Suzhou and is considered a benchmark of Jiangsu culinary artistry.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central freshwater fish (cooked) data with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, starch coating, and sweet-sour sauce composition. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored adaptation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore rated 'medium' per Project Veritas v2 policy.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (freshwater fish, cooked) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption (~+3–4 g fat per 100 g), starch coating carbohydrate contribution, and sweet-sour sauce sugar and sodium load. No exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g per 100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g per 100 g), and cholesterol (55 mg per 100 g) are estimated from USDA data for deep-fried freshwater fish; these fields were not present in the input record. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g — confirmed negligible for vegetable oil deep-frying at standard temperatures. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this deep-fried preparation with sauce is not available in USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on battered/crumbed fried fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in any approved source. Glycemic load per full 300 g serving is 29.8 g (high); calculated as (62 × 16.0 g net carb per 100 g × 3) / 100. 6. Duplicate portion entries in the input record (four identical 300 g entries) have been consolidated to a single canonical serving of 300 g. 7. The input sources (Yangzhou University, Suzhou University) are not on the approved source list per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md and have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved nutrient anchor. 8. Halal status is listed as false because the dish may contain rice wine or non-halal-certified ingredients depending on preparation; this should be verified for specific commercial preparations. 9. FoodOn mapped to closest_parent 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) and FoodEx2 mapped to closest_parent 'Fish, freshwater, cooked' (A0CKP); no exact codes exist for this dish; ontology gap notes are provided. 10. Vitamin A value (45.0 mcg RAE per 100 g) is plausible for fish with tomato-based sauce contribution; retained from input.
